title: Decision Filters

content.question: What standing rule would save you 20 decisions this month?
sop: Identify a category of recurring decisions, what to say yes to, what to buy, what projects to take
Write one explicit rule that pre-decides the category (e.g., 'no client calls after 5pm')
Rate how many options the rule needs to cut through (see calculation, page 5)
Post the rule somewhere visible, calendar, doc, wall, so it's enforceable, not just remembered
Enforce it for 30 days before adjusting it
content.explainer: A filter only saves effort if it's specific enough to actually eliminate options before you evaluate them, a vague value like 'be selective' still leaves you weighing every choice individually. The whole point of pre-committing is that the decision happens once, in the rule, instead of every single time the situation shows up again.
questions: What standing rule would save you 20 decisions this month?
Which category of decision do you currently make from scratch every time, when a simple rule would settle it instantly?
What's a rule you already believe in but haven't actually written down or enforced?
content.equation_example: Options: 12 choices you're weighing. Filter strength: rate 1-10 how strict your standing rule is. Effort = 12 divided by 8 = 1.5. Higher filter strength drives effort down sharply, fewer real decisions left to make; a weak filter (low number) barely cuts the options, so effort stays high even with a 'rule' in place.
content.closing_statement: An enforced rule beats ten you admire.

caption:
Principle Nº 213: Decision Filters

Pre-commit to simplifying choices. A filter only saves effort if it's specific enough to actually eliminate options before you evaluate them, a vague value like 'be selective' still leaves you weighing every choice individually.

Ask yourself: What standing rule would save you 20 decisions this month?
